ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for cream cheese tart or baked cheese tart; FOODON_03301710 (tart) is the closest broader class. Cheese tart is a filled pastry tart with cream cheese filling.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cheese tart / pastry with cheese filling — mapped to FoodEx2 group 'Pastries and cakes' under composite foods
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for baked cream cheese tart. A0FKP (pastries and cakes) is the closest defensible broader anchor. Specific cream cheese tart sub-code not available in FoodEx2 v2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to cream cheese tart as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a 3-piece set of signature cheese tarts, estimated at approximately 150 g total (approximately 50 g per individual tart), as sold by Singaporean specialty cheese tart retailers.

nutrient_source.title: 3Pcs Signature Cheese Tart (Original) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database, record G00010486
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per-100g nutrient values are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010486 for '3Pcs Signature Cheese Tart (Original)'. Confidence 0.95 as recorded in the source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1 where unavailable.

gi_source.title: Cheesecake (analogous estimate) — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283; University of Sydney GI Database.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a baked cream cheese tart. The GI of 46 is estimated by analogy to cheesecake (GI ~46, low range) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283). The cheese tart shares a similar cream cheese and pastry matrix with low net carbohydrate (8.85 g/100g). A GI of 46 places this product in the low GI category (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (46 × 13.275) / 100 ≈ 6.1 for a 150 g serving (3 tarts).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg/100g — more than double the 600 mg/100g clinical threshold for high-sodium foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this product; a single 3-piece serving (≈150 g) delivers approximately 1919 mg sodium, representing over 80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg, and poses a clinically significant acute blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero dietary fibre combined with high saturated fat impairs insulin sensitivity; net carbohydrate is moderate at 8.85 g/100g but the absence of fibre removes any buffering of glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid this product; the complete absence of dietary fibre, high fat content (23 g/100g), and high sodium (1279 mg/100g) collectively worsen glycaemic control and cardiovascular risk, and even small portions should be consumed only within a carefully managed meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (23 g/100g) with estimated high saturated fat from cream cheese and butter, combined with critically elevated sodium (1279 mg/100g) and cholesterol (78 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this product; the combination of high total fat, estimated saturated fat exceeding 10 g/100g from cream cheese and butter, sodium at 1279 mg/100g, and cholesterol at 78 mg/100g creates a compounded cardiovascular risk profile that is clinically contraindicated for regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High estimated saturated fat from cream cheese and butter promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines; sodium at 1279 mg/100g worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this product; the high saturated fat load from cream cheese and butter, combined with sodium at 1279 mg/100g and likely trans fat in the pastry shell, creates a strongly pro-inflammatory nutritional profile that may exacerbate joint inflammation and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Signature Cheese Tart (Original) is a premium baked pastry product sold as a 3-piece set by Singaporean specialty cheese tart retailers. Each individual tart consists of a short-crust or pâte sablée pastry shell filled with a warm, molten cream cheese custard made from cream cheese, butter, eggs, milk, and sugar, then baked until the filling is just set with a soft, lava-like centre. The texture contrast between the crisp, buttery pastry base and the rich, creamy, slightly tangy cheese filling is the defining characteristic of this product. Nutritionally, the cheese tart is calorie-dense at 307 kcal/100g, with a high fat content of 23 g/100g (predominantly from cream cheese and butter, with estimated saturated fat exceeding 10 g/100g), a moderate protein content of 16 g/100g from dairy proteins, and a low net carbohydrate of 8.85 g/100g. Dietary fibre is zero per the HPB source record. The most clinically notable feature is the exceptionally high sodium content of 1279 mg/100g, which is more than double the 600 mg/100g threshold for high-sodium foods and makes this product unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or fluid retention. Calcium is notably high at 1375 mg/100g, reflecting the cream cheese base. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 46 (low) by analogy to cheesecake per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, with a glycemic load of approximately 6.1 for the full 3-piece serving. This product is popular as a snack or teatime treat in Singapore and is associated with Japanese-inspired baked cheese tart trends that became widely adopted by Singaporean bakeries. It is not suitable for vegan, dairy-free, or low-sodium diets, and should be consumed only occasionally by the general population due to its high sodium, high saturated fat, and high caloric density.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cheese tart?
Is cheese tart suitable for hypertension?
How much fat in a baked cheese tart?
Signature cheese tart original nutritional value

confidence_reason: All core macronutrients and available micronutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010486 at confidence 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean food products. Saturated fat breakdown and several micronutrients are not reported in the HPB record and are documented as -1. GI is an analogous estimate from an approved GI source (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care) and is clearly documented as such.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for HPB record G00010486 is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat is estimated narratively at >10 g/100g based on cream cheese and butter composition for clinical guidance purposes only; this estimate is not entered as a numeric value in per_100g. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in HPB record G00010486 and are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this product as of 2026-04-16. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB record G00010486 — the HPB source explicitly reports 0.0 g dietary fibre per 100g for this product. 4. GI of 46 is an analogous estimate based on cheesecake GI values (GI ~46, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for baked cream cheese tart was found in approved sources. gi_category is 'low' consistent with GI 46 (low GI threshold: ≤55). Glycemic load for the 3-piece set (150 g serving) = (46 × 13.275) / 100 ≈ 6.1. 5. Halal status is not confirmed from the HPB record; is_halal is set to false pending verification of ingredient sourcing. 6. The product is associated with the Japanese-inspired baked cheese tart trend popularised in Singapore; cuisine is classified as Singaporean / Contemporary Asian Bakery.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, populated all mandatory sections, applied Singaporean multilingual naming rules.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2 per G5 honesty rule.

Iteration 3: Corrected glycemic_index from 55 to 46 and gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to resolve Q4 failure. GI 46 is the lower bound of the cheesecake analogue range per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, placing this product firmly in the low GI category (≤55). Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving from 7.3 to 6.1 accordingly. Updated gi_source notes and natural_language_description to reflect corrected GI value and category.
